questions.question: Implement a queue using a dynamic array in C. Include enqueue, dequeue, and resize operations. Show the complete code with proper memory management.
keyPoints: Define a struct with data pointer, front index, rear index, size, and capacity
Enqueue: check if full, resize by doubling if needed, insert at rear
Dequeue: check if empty, remove from front, optionally shrink at 1/4 capacity
Resize: allocate new array with double capacity, copy elements, free old array
Handle circular indexing or reset indices after resize

questions.question: Explain amortized analysis for the enqueue operation in a dynamic array-based queue. Prove that the amortized time is O(1) using the aggregate method.
keyPoints: Define amortized analysis as average cost over a worst-case sequence
Show that resize occurs at sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, ..., n/2
Total copy cost = n/2 + n/4 + n/8 + ... + 1 < n (geometric series)
Total cost for n operations = n (enqueues) + n (copies) = 2n
Amortized cost per operation = 2n / n = 2 = O(1)

questions.question: Compare queue implementation using static arrays, dynamic arrays, and linked lists. Discuss the time and space trade-offs of each approach.
keyPoints: Static array: O(1) enqueue/dequeue but fixed capacity, overflow possible
Dynamic array: amortized O(1) enqueue, O(1) dequeue, up to 50% space waste
Linked list: O(1) enqueue/dequeue with no capacity limit but pointer overhead per node
Cache performance: arrays (contiguous memory) outperform linked lists
Practical recommendation based on usage pattern

questions.question: What is the significance of choosing a growth factor for dynamic arrays? Explain what happens if the array grows by a constant amount instead of doubling.
keyPoints: Doubling: total copies form a geometric series summing to less than n
Constant increment c: resize happens every c insertions, copying grows linearly
Total copies with constant growth = c + 2c + 3c + ... = O(n^2)
Amortized cost becomes O(n) per insertion instead of O(1)
Multiplicative growth is essential for amortized constant time

questions.question: Write a note on the thrashing problem in dynamic arrays. How can it be avoided when implementing shrinking?
keyPoints: Thrashing: repeated alternation between growing and shrinking near a boundary
Occurs when shrink threshold equals half capacity (inverse of growth trigger)
Each alternating insert/delete triggers O(n) resize
Solution: shrink at 1/4 capacity (not 1/2), creating a buffer zone
Also known as hysteresis in resizing policy
